title: JavaScript Operators Knowledge Check
description: Check your understanding of arithmetic, logical, bitwise, comparison, and equality operators.
author: DailyCoder

questions.explanation: The remainder operator returns what is left after division. 10 divided by 3 leaves a remainder of 1.
Multiplication has higher precedence than addition, so 3 * 4 runs first, then 2 is added.
When + has a string operand, JavaScript performs string concatenation.

questions.prompt: If `let count = 5`, what does `console.log(count++)` print first?
questions.explanation: Postfix increment returns the current value first, then increments the variable.

questions.prompt: Which expression checks whether `num` is even?
questions.explanation: An even number has a remainder of 0 when divided by 2.

questions.prompt: What does `&&` return when all operands are truthy?
questions.explanation: In JavaScript, && returns the first falsy value it finds, or the last value if all operands are truthy.
The || operator returns the first truthy value. 0 is falsy, so it returns "Guest".
A non-empty string is truthy. Double NOT converts it to the boolean true.
& is bitwise AND. && is logical AND.

questions.prompt: What happens to decimal parts during most bitwise operations in JavaScript?

options.text: They are rounded to the nearest integer
They are removed during 32-bit integer conversion

questions.explanation: JavaScript converts operands to 32-bit integers before bitwise operations, so decimal parts are removed.
Both values are strings, so JavaScript compares them lexicographically. "1" comes before "2".

questions.prompt: What happens in a relational comparison with `NaN`, such as `NaN > 1`?
questions.explanation: Relational comparisons with NaN evaluate to false.

questions.prompt: Which expressions evaluate to true? (Select all that apply.)
questions.explanation: Loose equality allows coercion and has a special rule for null and undefined. Strict equality checks type too.

options.text: Arrays cannot be compared in JavaScript
They are different array references in memory
Strict equality always returns false for arrays

questions.explanation: Arrays are reference types. Two separate array literals create two different references even if the contents match.

questions.prompt: Which equality operator should you use by default in modern JavaScript?
questions.explanation: Use strict equality by default because it compares both value and type without hidden coercion.
